artifacts.summary: Official Texas A&M AI, Technology Services, Division of Research, and CTE sources provided enough public evidence for narrow staged claims. Stale, login-gated, course-specific, and news/advisory sources were documented but not used as policy evidence.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
Best Practices for Generative AI in Research

artifacts.claimText: Texas A&M publishes official recommendations and guidance for responsible generative AI use at the university, scoped to benefits and risks including academic integrity, privacy, and ethical use considerations.

citation.citationTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Texas A&M guidance says everyone must follow data privacy and security guidelines when using generative AI to protect personal and institutional data.
artifacts.normalizedValue: AI users must follow data privacy and security guidance

citation.citationTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Texas A&M guidance says generative AI users must acknowledge nontrivial AI-generated content and avoid plagiarism, while faculty should provide clear course instructions about permissible AI uses.
artifacts.normalizedValue: AI disclosure and course guidance tied to academic integrity

citation.citationTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Texas A&M presents AI Use Categories as a starting point for student-instructor conversation, not as a one-size-fits-all policy or replacement for instructor judgment.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Texas A&M strongly advises against sole reliance on AI detection tools because of limitations including inaccuracy, bias, ease of circumvention, and rapid AI evolution.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Caution against sole reliance on AI detection tools

artifacts.sourceTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The purpose of this page is to provide recommendations and guidance on the responsible use of generative AI at Texas A&M University, ensuring its potential benefits are maximized while minimizing risks to academic integrity, privacy and other ethical use considerations.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Texas A&M frames this page as responsible-use guidance, not as a standalone formal policy.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Everyone must follow data privacy and security guidelines when using generative AI, protecting personal and institutional data.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The source uses mandatory language for data privacy and security guidance.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.citationTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: When using generative AI, users must acknowledge the use of nontrivial AI-generated content and avoid plagiarism. This includes properly citing AI-generated content in academic work and ensuring that AI-generated content does not violate academic integrity policies. The faculty should provide clear instructions about permissible AI uses in their courses.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The source combines user acknowledgment obligations with instructor course guidance.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.citationTitle: Use Guidelines and Ethics | Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The AI Use Categories give students and instructors a starting point for conversation about AI usage in a course. While these categories are not a one-size-fits-all policy, nor are they meant to replace instructor judgment, they offer a shared way to think about different types of AI use.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The page explicitly avoids treating the AI categories as a universal policy.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Although AI detection tools are available, the university strongly advises against sole reliance on these tools due to the following limitations: Inaccuracy, Bias, Ease of Circumvention, Rapid Evolution of AI.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The page recommends caution and a broader integrity approach rather than sole detector use.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Challenges with AI Detection Tools section
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Open to all Texas A&M students and employees, TAMU AI Chat (currently in BETA) provides staff, faculty, researchers, and students with a secure, university-approved platform to access multiple AI tools like OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude Sonnet, and Google's Gemini. TAMU AI Chat supports content classified as University-Confidential or lower.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The service page names TAMU AI Chat as university-approved and gives the supported data classification.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: 3. (Recommended) Pursue a hybrid of the two previous options, including both of the following: a. An addition to the minimum syllabus requirements, which both i. makes explicit the responsibility of instructors and students to establish clear expectations for generative AI use within each course and/or assignment and ii. reinforces that the use of generative AI in academic coursework is integrally related to academic integrity and will be governed by the Aggie Honor Code. b. Guidance provided to support faculty in making their individual determinations.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The PDF recommends a hybrid syllabus approach and ties coursework AI use to academic integrity and the Aggie Honor Code.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Possible Courses of Action for the Institution
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: The claim is limited to the page purpose and does not treat all guidance text as binding policy.
Mandatory wording is supported by the source text.
The claim preserves the source distinction between must for users and should for faculty.
The claim is deliberately scoped to the categories page language.
The claim avoids saying AI detectors are banned.
The claim is limited to the Technology Services description and data classification.
The claim does not extend approval to all AI tools.
The claim is scoped to the Division of Research memo language.
The claim uses recommends because the source labels the option recommended.

artifacts.title: Texas A&M University Generative AI Policy Report - 2026-05-14
artifacts.summary: Texas A&M University has public official guidance for responsible generative AI use, student and teaching expectations, AI detection caution, Technology Services AI tools and data restrictions, and research best practices. Claims are staged candidates only and remain scoped to the cited source language.
limitations: This staged bundle is based only on public official sources retrieved on 2026-05-14.
The SharePoint AI Ethics and Governance Working Group report was not accessed because it is login-gated or institution-controlled.
The Graduate and Professional School AI theses PDF URL returned a 404 page and was not used.
Course-specific syllabi and official news/advisory pages were not used for university-wide policy claims.
This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
